lesson.title: Graphing Systems
lesson.body: To solve a system of linear equations **graphically**, graph each line on the same axes. The solution is the **intersection point**. Three cases: one intersection point = **one solution** (consistent/independent); lines are parallel (same slope, different intercepts) = **no solution** (inconsistent); lines are identical = **infinitely many solutions** (consistent/dependent). Always convert to slope-intercept form first to graph easily.

rooms.question: Two lines: y = x + 1 and y = −x + 5. Where do they intersect?
rooms.explanation: Set equal: x+1 = −x+5 → 2x = 4 → x = 2. Then y = 2+1 = 3. Intersection: (2, 3). Check in second line: −2+5 = 3 ✓.

rooms.question: Which system has no solution?
rooms.explanation: y = 3x + 1 and y = 3x + 5 both have slope 3 but different intercepts — they are parallel and never intersect. Option C (y = x+2 and 2y = 2x+4 → y = x+2) is actually the same line (infinite solutions). Options A and D have different slopes and do intersect.

rooms.question: How many solutions does the system y = 4x − 3 and 8x − 2y = 6 have?
rooms.explanation: Rewrite 8x − 2y = 6: −2y = −8x + 6 → y = 4x − 3. This is identical to the first equation. Every point on y = 4x − 3 is a solution, so there are infinitely many solutions. The system is dependent.

rooms.question: Estimate the intersection of y = (2/3)x − 1 and y = −x + 4.
rooms.explanation: Set equal: (2/3)x − 1 = −x + 4. Add x to both sides: (5/3)x = 5 → x = 3. Then y = −3+4 = 1. Intersection: (3, 1). Check: (2/3)(3)−1 = 2−1 = 1 ✓.

rooms.question: Match each system to its number of solutions (order: no solution, one solution, infinite solutions):
items: y = x + 2 and y = −x + 4 → one solution (intersect at (1,3))
rooms.explanation: Parallel lines (same slope, different intercepts) → no solution. Distinct intersecting lines → one solution. The same line written differently → infinite solutions. Simplify 2y=4x+6: y=2x+3, identical to the second equation.

boss.name: The Intersection Daemon
boss.taunt: Your lines will never meet mine.

questions.question: Solve graphically: y = 2x − 4 and y = −x + 2. Where is the intersection?
questions.explanation: Set equal: 2x−4 = −x+2 → 3x = 6 → x = 2. Then y = 2(2)−4 = 0. Intersection: (2, 0). Check: −2+2 = 0 ✓. This point lies on both lines.

questions.question: A system of two linear equations in two variables can have at most how many solutions?
questions.explanation: Two lines in a plane either: don't intersect (parallel — no solution), intersect at one point (one solution), or are the same line (infinitely many solutions). There is no case with exactly 2 solutions — two distinct lines can only cross at one point.

questions.question: Convert to slope-intercept and determine: does 3x + 2y = 8 and 6x + 4y = 20 have a solution?
answers: No — they are parallel (same slope, different intercepts)
Infinitely many — they are the same line
questions.explanation: Line 1: y = −(3/2)x + 4. Line 2: y = −(3/2)x + 5. Same slope (−3/2), different intercepts (4 vs 5). They are parallel — no solution. If line 2 had been 6x+4y=16 (i.e., 2× equation 1), they would be the same line.
